CURRENT AFFAIRS.

General .Carson is beginning to talk like a piece of
legal parchment now. He may shortly be referring to
““the said Covenant’’ and to ‘* his. executors, sdmin-
istrators and assigns,”’ and other parchment phrases of
that -kind. The Covenunt is nothing if not cautious:
The General advances, apparently carefully keeping his
retreat open.  If the Irish Parliament is established
““ we refuse to recognise its authority.””  Is that all,
Greneral ? >

And when Home Rule'is established, and when the

TImperial Tax collector has gone to one of the wealthy

" braves for the ‘monay, will the latter explain to a

_ public enquirer that of course he paid the tax, as if he
didn’t his furniture would be seized?

The Solemn Covenant is a solemn farce, and the
Orangemen supply first class comedy because. they are
that dull that they take the farce seriously.

We have italicised some words.

General Carson is, we believe, a lifelong dyspeptic,
and it is his liver and not his spirit that he is treating
Treland to just now. He has no condemnation, so far
as we haye read, of the awful atrocities in Belfast and
the forbidding of honest men and women because they
in the exercise of their religious, and theoretically civil,
liberty elect to be Catholics to earn their bread by the
sweat of their brow. At Lisburn his liver—to put the
most charitable if far-fetched explanation on it—said
that the North-Easterners including the Belfast man-
roasters, and *“ Covenanters ” in other parts of Ulster,
were the only democracy in Ireland! TPoor democracy,
whatever it may be, is in sad plight, at that rate. The
dyspeptic General asked, ** Where else is there a’ de-
mocracy, where else is there a free opinion?’’  Nay,
General, where else is a man carried to a furnace for
his opinions, where else are men beaten and intimidated
from earning their bread because of their opinions?

When the dyspeptic General read the Covenant to
Lisburn, he, as leader of the party that includes the
blackguards of Belfast who have assaulted men and
prevented, by fear of violence, men from: earning their
living and supporting themselves and those depending
on them, said:—

Ladies and gentlemen, that is the most solemn.
step that a free, a God-fearing, and law-abiding
people have ever been asked to take in defence of
their civil and religious liberty.

God-fearing and law-abiding indeed.

This dyspeptic public scoundrel who is stirring up
the low blackguards of Belfast, talked, in part of his
speech, like a cautious lawyer. He said:—

You won’t shirk from it? (No.) And I won’t
shirk from it. (Cheers.) Why are we compelled
to take this extreme step—a step, mind you,
which will pledge you and me to do everything
that is necessary, no matter what may be the con-
sequences to yourselves ? We pledge ourselves to
take all such steps as will be material effectually
for cver to put an end to this which I have de-
scribed before as a conspiracy against the elemen-
zm‘y rights of the citizenship in which we were

orn.

There is a wide field for quibbling and logic-chopping
there owing to the words we have italicised.

Here is another piece of rhethorvic that smells of
parchment
As to others, who say we are not going far
enough, I can only say this, that in my opinion this
Solemn Covenant will bind us to go forward, step
by step, not recklessly, but with grave considera-
tion of the consequences in every step that is
necessary to rid us and free us from this tyrannical
action of the Government dependent upon Irish
Nationalists—(hear, hear)—and, for my part, I
hope we will do it all discreetly and with calm
consideration and care.

' Of cowse if on
‘“ grave consideration '’ it is decided that the lambs
must grin and bear the law, then no step will be
‘“necessary,”’ acquiescence will be the word.

The future ** sacrifices "’ are now being set out with
lawyer-like ingenuity accompanied by the element of

7

THE LEADER.

nt bombast for consumption on the field. Here
& specimen i —

* But I promise you with all the steadfast con-
fidence that you and T feel towards each other,
that if this poliey is persisted in, there is no length
that may be necessary, no sacrifice thal may be
compulsory, that I and others who are associated
with me will not be prepared to ftake.

The italicised words show to us how patenfly this
lawyer mountebank is keeping open his retreat. *‘ May
be necessary,” and ‘“may be compulsory,’”’ are two
phrases supplying many loopholes.

Arnott’s *‘ Irish Times ” had a leading article after
Enniskillen, but before the Covenant saw the light: it
was a bit too previous, as the Yankees say, and so when
the Covenant was published it editorially shut up.

The ‘‘ Covenant '’ is not in harmony with the follow-
ing from the *‘ Irish Times '’ :—

The men who assembled at Enniskillen yester-
day are ready, if need be, to resist physical in-
vasion by physical means. That is the vital fact
of the prese ituation, and a very solemn and
terrible fact it i The crowd which listened to
Sir Edward Carson was a crowd drilled, through
its clubs and societies, for the emergencies of war.

By the way, in the whole course of the leading article
in Arnott’s paper we only came across one ‘‘ grim.’”

Arnott and his man, the ‘‘ saved '’ Healy, appears
to be going ahead of the ‘‘ Covenant’’ rabble, for
Arnott breathes “ civil war ’’ whilst Carson appears to
have fallen back on parchment. Arnott’s paper
says:—

The really important thing is that, for the
reasons which: Sir Edward Carson and Lord Hugh
Cecil stated yesterday, Ulster will refuse to accept
this Bill. It can only be thrust upoh the most
virile and most prosperous community in the
Empire at the cost of civil war.

Indeed Arnott’s paper on the day after Enniskillen

went in for a regular bout of “ civil war.”” Tt was quite

intoxicated with the sniff of ‘“civil war,”” for again in
the same article it said:—

If the present demonstrations convinee England

that she must not allow herself to be dragged into

the criminal adventure of civil war, they will have

served a great purpose; the situation will be re-
lieved by the Government’s fall from power. But,
if Mr. Redmond has his way, and 14110.()0\’01'1]111(311&
attempts to force this Home Rule Bill on Ulster,
then Ulster will be ready to resist ib.
No wonder the *“ Trish Times’’ had no editorial the
following day on the ‘ Covenant,” but that its first
leader was on—The Panama Canal !

The “ Trish Times " took a full day to think over
the wee Covenant, and on Saturday it did its best, and
that was not much, to put the best face it knew how

- on the wee thing. The wee Covenant is of course only

one of our old friends—a resolution. It is many years
ago since we first made Nationalist Treland sce th
ludicrousness of freeing Ireland with platoons of
unanimous resolutions, Poor old backward Ulﬁtcr is
now being led to believe, or muke‘l)c]icvg, in the
potency of winning battles with resolutions, and
Carson, the lawyer, has produced a Y\'cc“thmg
in that line which the ignorant ‘‘grims™ are
to be made feel, or pretend to {feel, is a
great weapon in their campaign. Nationalists
have found out the futility of fighting with unanimous
resolutions, and the poor ‘‘ grims’ are now being led
to battle armed with a wee ™ Covenant.”

The * Trish Times’’ on Saturday had the job of ex-
plaining away the wee Covenant. It wrote one ex-
quisite sentence of unconscious humour that we can-
not refrain from quoting:—‘ It (the British Govern-
ment) has sown the dragon’s teeth of the Home Rule
Bill, and they have sprung up as the armed men of
Derry and Inniskillen and Belfast.” That is cer-
tainly a fine raimeis tribute to the mounted foot!
Arnotl’s paper in writing about the poor wee Covenant
evidently decided to add a bit of powder and shot of
its own that was not in the wee text. So it said:—
“ Tt is no exaggeration, thercfore, to say that this Bill
can only be imposed on Ulster at the cost of civil war.””
But the wee Covenant said nothing about civil war.
Lawyer Carson knows better: something more than
even his furniture might be seized if he played at that
game.

Some time ago a friend called our attention to the
then current issue of *“ Stubbs’ Weekly Gazette.” The
reason he did so was that whilst all the brag and bluster
about ‘“ Ulster ”’ was going on the failures notified in
that week’s *“ Stubb were all, or nearly all, in the
North. We mislaid that interesting copy of *“ Stubbs,”
and so there was no paragraph about it.  Another
friend drew our attention to ‘* Stubbs' of September
18th, the day that Carson of the tears held forth at
Enniskillen. ~ There are twelve cases of failures noted
under the headings, * Lists of Creditors '’ and ‘‘ Notes
in Connection with Meetings of Creditors,”” ana of
these six are from Belfast and Derry, whilst one of the
smashes in Dublin represents a person who, we under-
stand, originally hailed from Belfast. Under the head-
ing, ‘ Bankruptey Meetings,”” we find three items, and
ol these two are from, of all places in the world—Ier-
managh, the capital of which is Enniskillen. Belfast’s
bluffers, bankrupts and blackguards are a pretty trio.
““ We will not ha’ Home Rule ~’ may be all very well,
but why go ‘ broke ” so-often?

the total income from excursion being over £114, that

- The report on Exporls and Imports from and to
. Ireland shows an increase of trade for the year 1911.
But why does not the ‘ Irish Times ' get someone
- with sense to write its editorial note on the matter.
Tt says:—'‘ The traflic in imports and exports is the
most trustworthy index to the prosperity of the coun-
try.”” Indeed it is nothing of the sort: as it so hap-
pens in the case of Ireland it is startling evidence of
our abnormal and undeveloped condition, and the only
consolation it gives to us is that it supplies evidence
of the big maxgin there is to work on when under Home
Rule, Ircland can'go full steam ahead towards normal
industrial conditions.  The colossal ignoramus who
writes editorially in the * Trish Times” on the matter,
remarks :—

The total extent of the import and export trade
per head of the population, according to the pre-
sent returns, places Ireland in a position vastly
superior to that of Great Britain on the same basis.

It is a calamity to have foolish things like the above
in the editorial columns of a Dublin daily paper. The
amount of import and export Irish trade per head of
the population is gn indication of our backward and
undeveloped industrial state.  We are sending out
millions of more or less raw material upon which little
industry or labour has been expended, and we are im-
porting millions’ worth of highly wrought goods in ex-
change. That marks a condition of most undesirable
lack of development. A normal industrial Ireland
would be manufacturing a huge amount of what it now
imports, it would be exporting far less stuff in the raw :
and the exports and imports might be far less per head
of the population whilst the wealth and industrial nor-
mality of the country had considerably increased.

who is the correspondent who can explain through the

ported aud explained by the ‘‘ Freeman,’”’ and by the
*“ Freeman ' alone. Where may they now look for
help, when the Irish Press approves the detaching of

dinary explanation that has ever appeared in the

import ov *‘ detach ’’ an English official to do so. Sir

business; Mr. Norway is profoundly ignorant of them,

complications when Home Rule comes. Should the
present P.M.G. not be there then (and he won’t) his
successor will not be bound by the appointment, and
the Home Rule Government will be saddled with an
Inglish imported official. Should the awful alterna-
tive happen and Home Rule not come, then Mr. Nor-
way can say with a pleasant smile, *‘ 'y swis et §’y
reste.””  There is no excuse or justification (except the*
““ Freeman’s’’ correspondent) for the appointment; it
does not make for efficiency, it will create complica-
tions, it is unjust to Irishmen, it is dangerous, useless
and ought to be abolished. Will the country stand it
or will the country act as gallant little Wales acted
and fling out an official who did not answer the national
requirements ? X

crowd at a football match, and you would see ten times
the enthusiasm.

Money was lavished in all directions to make the
display as imposing as possible. Decorations—such
as they were—were put up wherever they were allowed,
but it is not everywhere in Inniskillen that, Unionist
decorations find a place. I was going to add “‘ regard-
less of expense,’’ but that would be wrong, for a notice
appeared in the local papers from headquarters exhort-
ing cveryone to use bunting, adding as an inducement
that cheap cotton bunting would do quite well enough.
Cheap fares by special trains were arranged, and doubt-
less if the money were not forthcoming by the travellers
it would be provided for otherwise. Indeed the trains
provided the major portion of the meeting, which was
gathered from every corner of Ulster, wherever an
Orange lodge could scrape a few of its members to-
gether.  The local Unionist papers calculated that
20,000 people were present, and that 12,000 of these
came by train. The ‘‘ Freeman’’ reporter put it ab
7,000, which was much nearer the mark. Suppose, on
a generous estimate, that 10,000 were present, and
half of these came from outside. Half of the remainder,
at least, were women, so that about 2,500 Fermanagh
men turned up to welcome Carson. Considering the
furious whipping up indulged in previous to the meet-
ing, this result must have been a distinet disappoint-
ment to the promoters.

The platform was calculated to hold 1,000 people.

It was nothing of the kind. ~ About one-third of the
seating space was empty all the time. The ‘‘hard-
headed ** Ulster farmers did not see their way to spend
half-a-crown for the glory of sitting beside the dauntless
leader.

trast between expectations and results. Vast stores
of eatables and drinkables were laid in, tents were
erected, and the rector of Enniskillen expected quite
a_windfall for his parish fund out of the proceeds.
Alas! the tents were empty, the bottles unopened, the
buns untasted, comparatively speaking. The buns
were brought down in carts to the back streets of the
town that evening, and sold at the ruinous price of

bition of force, they were intensely comic. A penny
cracker judiciously placed by a small boy would have
put the entire squadron—white-haired old men, beard-
less boys, cart-horses, plough-horses, fooisore old
trammers and windbroken old racers—out of action.
“Trimble’s trembling troops’” would provide A. M. W.
with delightful copy.

The ““drill ”” was conspicuous by its absence. The
various bands ‘‘marched ’’ to their own music—
Heavens ! what a diabolical noise they made! Nothing
but a centipede could keep step to such time, and the
““march ” was a mixture of shuffling and shambling.
The bands are a holy terror. Not one of them knows
how to play the ‘“ Boyne Water.”” They play it like
a funeral march, and 1t is really a fine tune when pro-
perly rendered.
